label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out what the main topic of the passage is. Let me start by reading through the text again to make sure I understand it properly. The passage begins by talking about how both men and women are surrounded by magazines and websites that focus on dieting, exercise, fashion, and hairstyles. It mentions that in the past, men only had to worry about wearing a nice suit to be considered smart and handsome, but times have changed. Then it references a survey by the University of the West of England (UWE) that found 80% of men are unhappy with their bodies, specifically pointing out issues like beer bellies and lack of muscles. The text suggests that this increased concern about body image might be due to the influence of TV and magazine role models, which set a standard for the "perfect" body. However, not everyone is willing to put in the effort to achieve that, so there's even an inventor who made a muscle-enhancing shirt to make men look more muscular. Joe Warner, a former editor of a men's fitness magazine, criticizes this product as a gimmick and argues that people should focus on working out instead of taking shortcuts. The passage then states that while exercise is good, one shouldn't forget that character is more important than appearance. The author concludes by saying they might go to the gym before heading out to the pub, not aiming for a six-pack but to look healthier and boost confidence. The final question is about how much the reader worries about their physique. Now, the question is asking what the passage mainly talks about, and the options are A to D. Let me look at the options again: A) You shouldn't judge a book by its cover. B) More and more people become obese in England. C) Role models put great pressure on common people. D) Men become more and more conscious of their body image. I need to determine which of these is the main topic. Let's break it down. The passage starts by discussing how both men and women are influenced by media that promotes certain body ideals.
